items.title: Rapid growth and a new ransomware variant
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service group emerged as a top-10 threat actor in the first half of 2026. The group exploits vulnerabilities in internet-facing devices like VPNs and firewalls, potentially collaborating with initial access brokers. They employ comprehensive reconnaissance using tools like SharpADWS, NetScan, and Advanced IP Scanner, capturing network traffic with netsh. The attackers
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service group has emerged as a significant threat, exploiting vulnerabilities in internet-facing devices such as VPNs and firewalls to gain initial access. They utilize tools like SharpADWS, NetScan, and Advanced IP Scanner for comprehensive reconnaissance, and capture network traffic using netsh. If exploited, this can lead to data encryption and significant impact, particularly for organizations with sensitive data. To remediate, defenders should ensure all internet-facing devices are up-to-date with the latest security patches, and implement robust network segmentation and monitoring. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a robust backup and disaster recovery plan to mitigate the impact of potential ransomware attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1650| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1588| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From Bing Search to Ransomware: Bumblebee and AdaptixC2 Deliver Akira
items.description: In July 2025, threat actors compromised organizations through SEO poisoning campaigns targeting users searching for legitimate IT management tools. Users downloading trojanized installers for ManageEngine OpManager received Bumblebee malware, granting initial access. The attackers exploited the fact that users executing these IT tools were privileged administrators, enabling rapid lateral movement
items.ai_summary: The Bumblebee malware campaign exploited SEO poisoning tactics, specifically T1608.006, to target users searching for legitimate IT management tools, leading to the compromise of organizations. The attackers distributed trojanized installers for ManageEngine OpManager, which, when executed by privileged administrators, granted initial access and enabled rapid lateral movement. If exploited, this vulnerability can result in data encryption for impact, as identified by T1486, with real-world examples showing the potential for significant disruption to organizational operations. To remediate, defenders should implement strict controls on software downloads, ensuring that only trusted sources are used, and monitor for suspicious activity related to remote services (T1021) and application layer protocols (T1071). Additionally, defenders should prioritize patching and securing ManageEngine OpManager to prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608.006| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Chromium extension uses AI‑related branding to redirect browser search
items.description: Microsoft Threat Intelligence identified a malicious Chromium extension spoofing Perplexity AI to deceive users into installation. The extension's primary objective involves search traffic interception and data collection through Manifest Version 3 capabilities and declarativeNetRequest rules. It routes both full search queries and real-time keystrokes through attacker-controlled infrastructure ho
items.ai_summary: The malicious Chromium extension, masquerading as Perplexity AI, exploits users' trust by leveraging AI-related branding to gain installation access. This extension utilizes Manifest Version 3 capabilities and declarativeNetRequest rules to intercept search traffic and collect data, including full search queries and real-time keystrokes, which are then routed through attacker-controlled infrastructure. The real-world impact of this exploit involves the compromise of sensitive user data, potentially affecting individuals and organizations relying on Chromium-based browsers. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately remove the malicious extension and update their browser to the latest version, ensuring that Manifest Version 3 capabilities are properly secured. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing extensions, verifying the authenticity and legitimacy of the developer and the extension's permissions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1056| C[Input Capture]:::tactic
    C -->|T1114| D[Email Collection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1005| E[Data from Local System]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: RAT Abuses TON Blockchain to Target Japan's Hotel Industry
items.description: A sophisticated phishing campaign observed in May 2026 targets Japanese accommodation facilities partnering with Booking.com. Attackers impersonate guest complaints and review requests through emails, tricking hotel staff into downloading malicious ZIP files containing shortcut links disguised as photos. The malware, TONResolver, employs The Open Network blockchain platform as a dead drop resolver
items.ai_summary: The TONResolver malware is being distributed through a sophisticated phishing campaign targeting Japanese hotels, specifically those partnered with Booking.com. The attackers send emails impersonating guest complaints and review requests, tricking hotel staff into downloading malicious ZIP files containing shortcut links disguised as photos. If exploited, the malware can lead to unauthorized access and potential data breaches, affecting the hotel industry's customer data and operational systems. To remediate, defenders should implement email filtering rules to block suspicious emails with ZIP file attachments, and ensure all staff are trained to identify phishing attempts. Additionally, hotels should verify the authenticity of guest complaints and review requests through alternative channels, such as direct phone calls or official Booking.com communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1102| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Chrome and Firefox Extensions Posing as Free VPNs Add Clipboard Stealers via Malicious Updates
items.description: Malicious browser extensions distributed through Chrome Web Store and Firefox Add-ons marketplaces posed as free VPN services while secretly stealing clipboard data. The Chrome extension, with 146 users, and Firefox extension, with 3,499 users, initially functioned as proxy tools but later incorporated clipboard theft through staged updates. Chrome versions 1.1 onwards and Firefox version 1.3.3 on
items.ai_summary: The malicious Chrome and Firefox extensions, posing as free VPN services, utilized the T1176 tactic by distributing through the Chrome Web Store and Firefox Add-ons marketplaces. These extensions, with 146 and 3,499 users respectively, initially functioned as proxy tools but later incorporated clipboard theft through staged updates, leveraging the T1115 tactic to steal sensitive data. The Chrome extension, versions 1.1 onwards, and Firefox extension, version 1.3.3, are specifically affected. To remediate, users should immediately remove the extensions and update their browsers to the latest versions, ensuring that any clipboard access permissions are revoked. Furthermore, defenders should implement strict extension validation and monitoring to prevent similar incidents, and consider utilizing browser extension whitelisting to restrict the installation of untrusted extensions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1176| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1115| C[Collection]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A Djinn in the Machine: TaskWeaver's Node.js Intrusion Chain
items.description: An intrusion was investigated that began with exploitation of CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in SimpleHelp RMM software. The threat actor obtained unauthorized technician access and deployed two previously undocumented malware samples: TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer. TaskWeaver is a heavily obfuscated Node.js loader that establishes encrypted communications and delive
items.ai_summary: The threat actor exploited CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in SimpleHelp RMM software, to gain unauthorized technician access. This vulnerability allows attackers to bypass authentication mechanisms, potentially leading to a full system compromise. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the deployment of two previously undocumented malware samples: TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update their SimpleHelp RMM software to the latest version and ensure that all technician accounts have strong passwords and multi-factor authentication enabled. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for signs of TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer malware, such as unusual Node.js activity or encrypted communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Unscheduled Task]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Defence Impairment Olympics
items.description: A sophisticated attack sequence was detected beginning June 7 involving a steganographically hidden webshell on a vulnerable Adobe ColdFusion server. The threat actor executed extensive enumeration commands before deploying approximately a dozen defence impairment techniques. These included disabling IIS logging, tampering with Microsoft Defender, timestomping file metadata, killing Sysmon and Fil
items.ai_summary: The Defence Impairment Olympics attack sequence began with the exploitation of a vulnerable Adobe ColdFusion server, where a steganographically hidden webshell was deployed, classified as T1505.003. The threat actor then executed extensive enumeration commands, followed by the deployment of approximately a dozen defence impairment techniques, including disabling IIS logging and tampering with Microsoft Defender, which can be attributed to T1070. To mitigate this threat, defenders should immediately update their Adobe ColdFusion servers to the latest version and implement robust logging and monitoring, including enabling Sysmon and File Integrity Monitoring. Additionally, defenders should conduct regular audits to detect any suspicious activity, such as timestomping file metadata, and implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent webshell deployments.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1505.003| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1070| D[Defence Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1070| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: How access to Gmail accounts is gained
items.description: The ToddyCat APT group developed a sophisticated tool called Umbrij to compromise Gmail corporate accounts through OAuth token theft. The malware exploits Chromium-based browsers by launching them in headless mode with remote debugging enabled, utilizing the Shadow Token via Remote Debug (STRD) technique. Umbrij automates the entire attack chain: it copies user profiles, launches browsers with deb
items.ai_summary: The ToddyCat APT group has developed a sophisticated tool called Umbrij to compromise Gmail corporate accounts through OAuth token theft. This malware exploits Chromium-based browsers by launching them in headless mode with remote debugging enabled, utilizing the Shadow Token via Remote Debug (STRD) technique. The real-world impact of this exploit is significant, as it allows attackers to gain unauthorized access to sensitive email accounts, potentially leading to data breaches and other malicious activities. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that their Chromium-based browsers are updated to the latest version and that remote debugging is disabled. Additionally, users should be cautious when granting OAuth tokens to third-party applications and monitor their account activity regularly.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1134| B[Access Token Manipulation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: India's government and energy sectors targeted with ZOHOMURK and MINIRECON
items.description: Mustang Panda orchestrated two concurrent espionage campaigns targeting Indian government entities and hydropower infrastructure between May and June 2026. The campaigns leveraged DLL sideloading via legitimate executables to deploy newly identified malware including SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and ZOHOMURK. MINIRECON represents an evolution of Toneshell with WebSocket-based command-and-control capabi
items.ai_summary: The Mustang Panda threat actor has been observed conducting two concurrent espionage campaigns targeting Indian government entities and hydropower infrastructure between May and June 2026. The campaigns leveraged DLL sideloading via legitimate executables to deploy newly identified malware, including SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and ZOHOMURK, with MINIRECON representing an evolution of Toneshell featuring WebSocket-based command-and-control capabilities. If exploited, these campaigns could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information and potential disruption of critical infrastructure, affecting government operations and energy supply. To remediate, defenders should implement robust application whitelisting, monitor for suspicious DLL loading, and ensure up-to-date endpoint security solutions are in place. Additionally, network traffic should be inspected for unusual WebSocket communications to detect potential command-and-control activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1574.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A stealthy RAT burrowing deep into Android devices
items.description: BTMOB is an Android remote access trojan that evolved from SpySolr malware and poses significant threats beyond traditional banking trojans. The malware combines phishing-led delivery with an APK builder interface that enables rapid payload generation without coding skills. Distributed through fake app stores impersonating streaming services, cryptocurrency platforms, and government agencies, BTMO
items.ai_summary: The BTMOB Android remote access trojan (RAT) is a stealthy malware that has evolved from the SpySolr malware, posing significant threats to Android devices. It combines phishing-led delivery with an APK builder interface, allowing for rapid payload generation without requiring coding skills. The malware is distributed through fake app stores that impersonate streaming services, cryptocurrency platforms, and government agencies, making it a significant threat to users who may unknowingly download the malicious app. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all Android devices have the latest security updates installed, and users should only download apps from trusted sources, such as the official Google Play Store. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on links or providing sensitive information, as phishing attacks are a primary delivery method for this malware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.description: A weakness has been identified in IM-Magic Partition Resizer up to 7.9.0. This affects an unknown function in the library MDA_NTDRV.sys of the component Kernel Driver. This manipulation causes improper access controls. The attack requires local access. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not r
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12784 is a vulnerability in IM-Magic Partition Resizer up to version 7.9.0, affecting the MDA_NTDRV.sys library in the Kernel Driver component, which leads to improper access controls. This weakness requires local access to exploit and can be used to gain unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. The exploit has been made publicly available, increasing the risk of attacks. To remediate, users should update to IM-Magic Partition Resizer version 7.9.1 or later, and ensure that all related kernel driver components are updated to the latest version. Additionally, defenders should monitor system logs for suspicious activity and restrict local access to authorized personnel only.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12782 is a security flaw in EaseUS Partition Master up to version 14.5, specifically in the EUEDKEPM.sys library of the Kernel Driver component, allowing for improper access controls. This vulnerability can be exploited locally, and an exploit has been publicly released, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data breaches. The real-world impact includes potential data theft and system compromise for users of affected EaseUS Partition Master versions. To remediate, defenders should upgrade to EaseUS Partition Master version 14.6 or later, ensuring the updated EUEDKEPM.sys library is installed. Additionally, users should monitor system logs for suspicious activity and restrict local access to sensitive data.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12781 is a local vulnerability in EaseUS Partition Master up to version 14.5, affecting the kernel driver epmntdrv.sys. The vulnerability allows for improper access controls, which can be exploited by an attacker with local access. If exploited, an attacker could potentially gain elevated privileges or execute arbitrary code. To remediate this vulnerability, users should upgrade to EaseUS Partition Master version 14.6 or later, which has been patched by the vendor. Additionally, users should ensure that the kernel driver is properly configured and up-to-date.

Executing a manipulation can lead to improper access controls. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been publicly disclosed and may be utilized. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12780 is a vulnerability in AOMEI Backupper up to version 8.3.0, specifically in the amwrtdrv.sys kernel driver library, which can lead to improper access controls when exploited. The attack requires local access to the system, and the exploit has been publicly disclosed, making it potentially accessible to malicious actors. If successfully exploited, this vulnerability could allow attackers to gain unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems, potentially affecting individuals and organizations relying on AOMEI Backupper for backup and data protection. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update AOMEI Backupper to a version later than 8.3.0, as the vendor has presumably addressed this issue in subsequent releases. Users can also consider alternative backup solutions or implement additional access controls to mitigate potential risks.

items.title: Anthropic to restore Claude Fable access on Wednesday
items.description: Anthropic has confirmed that the Department of Commerce has lifted export controls on Claude's two most powerful models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5. [...]

items.title: Anthropic rolls out Sonnet 5 with near-Opus 4.8 performance at a lower price
items.description: Anthropic is now rolling out Sonnet 5, and it's almost as good as the Opus range, but it is designed to be cheaper than the company's flagship model. [...]

items.title: New BioShocking attack manipulates AI browser into data theft
items.description: A new prompt injection attack dubbed "BioShocking" could trick AI-powered browsers into treating real-world risky actions as part of a fictional scenario, causing them to ignore any safety guardrails. [...]

items.title: Microsoft accelerates quantum-safe roadmap as risks grow
items.description: Microsoft announced today that it is accelerating its quantum-safe security roadmap, saying advances in quantum computing are bringing the need to replace today's encryption standards sooner than previously expected. [...]

items.title: Malicious PyPI packages give hackers control of Telegram bot servers
items.description: A campaign active since last November has been targeting Python developers building Telegram bots with trojanized Pyrogram forks that allow attackers to read arbitrary files on compromised servers. [...]

items.title: CIA chief highlights major shifts in agency’s tech approach
items.description: CIA Director John Ratcliffe said artificial intelligence capabilities are "akin to digital nuclear weapons.”

items.title: Microsoft Warns Poisoned MCP Tool Descriptions Can Make AI Agents Leak Data
items.description: New Microsoft research shows how attackers can hijack AI agents that act on a user's behalf, using nothing more than a poisoned tool description to make the agent quietly hand over company data to an outsider. The trick is that the agent never breaks a rule. Every step looks routine, so in a default setup no alarm may fire. The work comes from Microsoft Incident Response and its

items.title: RustDuck Botnet Rebuilds in Rust to Hijack Routers and Servers for DDoS
items.description: A new two-stage malware family called RustDuck is hijacking home routers, IP cameras, Android boxes, and poorly secured servers, then stitching them into a network built to knock websites and online services offline. Researchers at QiAnXin's XLab have tracked it since February 2026, and say the real story is not how big it is today, but how fast it is changing. The end goal is a

items.title: House passes kids’ online safety bill, but Senate approval unlikely
items.description: The Kids Internet and Digital Safety (KIDS) Act passed with bipartisan support by a 267-117 margin, winning the two-thirds majority needed to greenlight the legislation under a process that speeds up a bill’s path to a vote but requires more than a simple majority.

items.title: Langflow RCE Exploited to Deploy Monero Miner on Exposed AI App Endpoints
items.description: Threat actors are continuing to exploit a critical Langflow vulnerability as part of fresh attacks designed to deliver a Monero cryptocurrency miner. The activity has been found to weaponize CVE-2026-33017 (CVSS score: 9.3), an unauthenticated remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in Langflow, indicating threat actors are scanning and targeting exposed artificial intelligence (AI)

items.title: Fake Perplexity extension on Chrome Web Store tracked searches
items.description: A malicious extension in the Chrome Web Store is masquerading as the Perplexity AI answer engine, intercepting search traffic and collecting browsing information. [...]

items.title: Silent Swap Crypto Clipper Uses Fake Google Notes Extension to Replace Wallet Addresses
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged an active browser extension campaign that is designed to steal cryptocurrency by stealthily replacing wallet addresses when unsuspecting users initiate a transaction. The cryptocurrency clipper activity has been codenamed Silent Swap by McAfee Labs. "The campaign is delivered through unsigned installers – observed in both .NET and Golang variants – that

items.title: GuardFall Exposes Open-Source AI Coding Agents to Decades-Old Shell Injection Risks
items.description: The safety check that is supposed to stop an AI coding agent from running a dangerous command can be walked straight past using a shell trick that has been public for decades. New research from Adversa AI, which is named the bypass GuardFall, found it works against ten of the eleven popular open-source coding and computer-use agents the firm tested. Only one, "Continue," was built to

items.title: Lessons from the Underground: How to Combat Business Email Compromise
items.description: Business Email Compromise is more than an email scam. It's a coordinated operation involving compromised accounts, financial research, and cash-out networks. Flare explores how underground forums reveal how BEC attacks are planned and executed. [...]

items.title: BlueHammer Vulnerability Exploited in Ransomware Attacks
items.description: The Microsoft Defender vulnerability CVE-2026-33825 was exploited in the wild as a zero-day before patches were released. The post BlueHammer Vulnerability Exploited in Ransomware Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: An intelligence budget 'super user' job is now in the hands of Russ Vought
items.description: Russell Vought, director of the White House Office of Management and Budget (OMB), assumed hands-on responsibility for overseeing the spending plans of intelligence agencies following the recent departure of Amaryllis Fox Kennedy, a senior intelligence official who simultaneously served in multiple roles, including one at OMB.

items.title: 282 iOS AI Apps Leak API Keys and Open AI Proxy Access in Network Traffic Study
items.description: Researchers tested 444 AI chatbot apps for iPhone and found that 282 of them, nearly two-thirds, exposed paid AI access through their network traffic. In many cases, the path in was visible just by watching what the app sent: a plaintext API key, a reusable token, or a backend server that accepted requests with no key at all. Whoever grabs it can send model requests on the developer's account,

items.title: Decades-Old Bash Tricks Expose AI Coding Agents to Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: Decades-old Bash shell tricks can bypass safeguards in most open source AI coding agents, potentially turning malicious repositories into supply chain attack vectors. The post Decades-Old Bash Tricks Expose AI Coding Agents to Supply Chain Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Aflac Japan Data Breach Impacts 4.38 Million
items.description: Hackers accessed the insurance giant’s policyholder portal multiple times between June 15 and June 25. The post Aflac Japan Data Breach Impacts 4.38 Million appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Hacker Conversations: Chris Thompson, Former Head of IBM X-Force Red, Co-Founder of RemoteThreat
items.description: Chris Thompson's journey took him from hacking game controls as a teenager to founding IBM’s X-Force Red team. The post Hacker Conversations: Chris Thompson, Former Head of IBM X-Force Red, Co-Founder of RemoteThreat appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Supreme Court Rules Constitutional Privacy Protections Apply to Cellphone Users’ Location History
items.description: The ruling was made in the case of a bank robber whose identity was discovered through a geofence warrant. The post Supreme Court Rules Constitutional Privacy Protections Apply to Cellphone Users’ Location History appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: What the Numbers Say About FIFA 2026 Cyber Risk
items.description: The FIFA World Cup 2026 opened on June 11. By that date, according to Check Point Research, the fraud infrastructure targeting it had already been built, staged, and partially deployed. Threat actor activity was pre-planned, months out, across three sectors and at least ten languages. Check Point Exposure Management published the FIFA World Cup 2026 Cyber Threat Report this month, covering

items.title: Exploitation of Recent Oracle E-Business Suite Vulnerability Begins
items.description: The critical-severity defect allows unauthenticated attackers to take over the E-Business Suite’s Payments product. The post Exploitation of Recent Oracle E-Business Suite Vulnerability Begins appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Attackers Exploit SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558 to Deploy TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer
items.description: An unknown threat actor has been observed exploiting a recently disclosed maximum-severity security flaw in SimpleHelp to deliver two previously unreported malware families, TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer. The intrusion involves the exploitation of CVE-2026-48558 (CVSS score: 10.0), a critical authentication bypass vulnerability impacting the OpenID Connect (OIDC) flow that an unauthenticated

items.title: Insurance giant Aflac discloses data breach after subsidiary hack
items.description: American insurance giant Aflac has disclosed a new data breach after attackers breached its Japan subsidiary's systems and stole personal and bank account information. [...]

items.title: Microsoft adds smarter bot protection to Teams meetings
items.description: Microsoft has introduced a new Teams admin policy that allows organizers to prevent third-party bots from joining meetings without approval. [...]

items.title: The AI Token Costs That Can Break Cybersecurity
items.description: As cybersecurity platforms embrace agentic AI, organizations must balance detection performance against the escalating costs of token consumption, deployment architecture, and AI credits. The post The AI Token Costs That Can Break Cybersecurity appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Kali Linux 2026.2 released with 9 new tools, NetHunter updates
items.description: Kali Linux 2026.2, the second release of the year, is now available for download, featuring 9 new tools and numerous Kali NetHunter improvements. [...]

items.title: June 2026 Apple Updates, (Tue, Jun 30th)
items.description: Apple released updates for iOS/iPadOS, macOS, and Safari on Monday. There have been no updates for other Apple operating systems (visionOS, watchOS, tvOS). Usually, Apple updates all products at the same time.

items.title: AirDrop and Quick Share Flaws Let Nearby Attackers Trigger Crashes and Bypass Checks
items.description: Two researchers have found six security flaws in AirDrop and Quick Share, the wireless features that beam files between nearby devices with no cables or shared network. An attacker within wireless range, with just a laptop and no prior connection, can crash the sharing service on a Mac or iPhone set to receive from anyone, with no tap or prompt. The same research found Quick Share flaws that

items.title: Nissan Employee Data Breached in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack
items.description: Only a handful of the 100 organizations targeted in the PeopleSoft campaign have been confirmed. The post Nissan Employee Data Breached in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical SimpleHelp Vulnerability Exploited for Malware Delivery
items.description: The threat actor is focused on collecting credentials, SSH keys, cryptocurrency wallets, and development tooling. The post Critical SimpleHelp Vulnerability Exploited for Malware Delivery appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New BioShocking Attack Tricks AI Browsers Into Leaking User Credentials
items.description: Convince an AI browser that it is playing a game, and it can hand over your login details. That is the finding behind BioShocking, a technique from security firm LayerX that tricked six AI browsers and assistants into copying a user's credentials and sending them to an attacker. The targets included OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity's Comet, and Anthropic's Claude browser extension. An

items.title: Quantifind Raises $200 Million for AI-Native Risk Intelligence
items.description: Quantifind will accelerate international expansion and extend its platform’s localized risk intelligence capabilities. The post Quantifind Raises $200 Million for AI-Native Risk Intelligence appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 30th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9988, (Tue, Jun 30th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: 'Djinn' Stealer Targets Cloud, AI Credentials
items.description: The infostealer was delivered via CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in SimpleHelp, targeting credentials linking development and admin environments to wider enterprise systems.

items.title: Vulnerabilities Expose Private Data in Indian Government Systems
items.description: One critical vulnerability, among many discovered by a researcher, could have allowed anyone to walk in and take over a national government portal.

items.title: Can Clothes Make You Invisible to Facial Recognition?
items.description: Does life feel Orwellian sometimes? One researcher has a solution for you: graphic tees that confuse the neural networks in surveillance cameras.

items.title: Iran, Russia, China Target Water Systems for Sabotage
items.description: Nation-state attackers breach water systems through weak passwords, exposed PLCs, and poor segmentation — not sophisticated malware.

items.title: Justices rule that cellphone location histories are protected by the Fourth Amendment
items.description: Police must get a warrant to request geofence data involving individual cellphones, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in what represents a victory for privacy advocates.

items.title: US racks up about 400 wins over illegal World Cup streaming sites
items.description: The World Cup’s organizing body, FIFA, helped identify hundreds of domains taken down in an action organized by the U.S., along with the help of U.S. broadcaster NBC Universal and other entities.

items.title: Adding some Automation to the favicon.ico method of Host Recon, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;m in the throes of target host recon for another pentest, and thought I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;d share some workflow / automation stuff. In the past, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve discussed using historic DNS "mining" to collect target hosts in the domain.

items.title: Amazon Q VS Extension Flaw Leads to Cloud Credential Theft
items.description: Adversaries could plant a malicious repository that can execute arbitrary code and steal cloud credentials by exploiting the vulnerability, which showcases growing MCP risk.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 29th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9986, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: YARA-X 1.18.0 and 1.19.0 Release, (Sun, Jun 28th)
items.description: YARA-X&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s 1.18.0 release brings 3 improvements and 2 bugfixes.

items.title: Third-Party Breaches Teach Education Sector a Costly Lesson in Vendor Risk
items.description: Rising threats from third-party actors are forcing institutions to play defense to protect student data from ransomware and other attacks.

items.title: AI Decline? Confidence in Autonomous Penetration Testing Falls
items.description: Companies are still experimenting with automated AI systems to find security weaknesses, but fewer are relying on the technology.

items.title: Cisco Adds NHI to Security Stack With Astrix, WideField Acquisitions
items.description: Cisco joins a growing list of security platform providers that are betting that securing the agentic workforce means turning identity into the primary control plane.

items.title: New Initiative Tackles Security for End-of-Life Open Source Software
items.description: The Open Source Sustainability Initiative's goal is to help enterprises manage and secure aging open source projects while maintaining regulatory compliance.

items.title: AI Won't Wipe-Out Entry-Level Cybersecurity Jobs
items.description: Instead of eliminating jobs for early-career cyber pros, AI is creating new opportunities for candidates with strong human decision-making skills.

items.title: What do Ports Hear When Nobody's Listening? An Assessment of Automated Cybercrime [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 24th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9984, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Fixed the Bug. Nobody Fixed the Configuration., (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: The vulnerability

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
